mn10:1.2: at one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling among the Kurus, where there is a market town of the Kurus named Kammāsadhamma.
mn10:10.0: 1.4. Contemplation of Body: Attention to the Unattractive
mn10:10.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, upward from the soles of the feet and downward from the tips of the hairs, enclosed by skin and full of many kinds of impurities:
mn10:10.2: ‘In this body there are head hairs, body hairs, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, diaphragm, spleen, lungs, intestines, mesentery, stomach contents, feces, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, grease, saliva, mucus, synovial fluid, urine.’
mn10:10.3: Just as, *bhikkhus*, there were a bag with openings at both ends, full of various kinds of grain, such as hill rice, paddy rice, mung beans, peas, sesame seeds, and husked rice; and a person with good eyes, having opened it, were to review it: ‘These are hill rice, these are paddy rice, these are mung beans, these are peas, these are sesame seeds, these are husked rice’;
mn10:10.4: so too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, upward from the soles of the feet and downward from the tips of the hairs, enclosed by skin and full of many kinds of impurities:
mn10:10.5: ‘In this body there are head hairs, body hairs … urine.’
mn10:11.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally …
mn10:11.2: in this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:11.3: Attention to the unattractive section finished.
mn10:12.0: 1.5. Contemplation of Body: Attention to Elements
mn10:12.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, however it stands, however it is positioned, by way of elements:
mn10:12.2: ‘In this body there is the earth element, the water element, the fire element, and the air element.’
mn10:12.3: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a skilled butcher or a butcher’s apprentice, having slaughtered a cow and divided it into portions, were sitting at a crossroads;
mn10:12.4: so too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, however it stands, however it is positioned, by way of elements:
mn10:12.5: ‘In this body there is the earth element, the water element, the fire element, and the air element.’
mn10:13.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally …
mn10:13.2: in this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:14.0: 1.6. Contemplation of Body: Nine Charnel Grounds
mn10:14.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* might see a corpse thrown into a charnel ground, dead for one day, or dead for two days, or dead for three days, swollen, discolored, and festering.
mn10:14.2: He compares this very body to it:
mn10:14.3: ‘This body too has such a nature, will be such, has not gone beyond this.’
mn10:15.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally …
mn10:15.2: in this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:16.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* might see a corpse thrown into a charnel ground, being eaten by crows, or being eaten by hawks, or being eaten by vultures, or being eaten by herons, or being eaten by dogs, or being eaten by tigers, or being eaten by leopards, or being eaten by jackals, or being eaten by various kinds of creatures.
mn10:16.2: He compares this very body to it:
mn10:16.3: ‘This body too has such a nature, will be such, has not gone beyond this.’
mn10:17.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally …
mn10:17.2: in this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:18-23.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* might see a corpse thrown into a charnel ground, a skeleton with flesh and blood, held together by sinews …
mn10:18-23.2: a skeleton without flesh, smeared with blood, held together by sinews …
mn10:18-23.3: a skeleton with flesh and blood gone, held together by sinews …
mn10:2.1: “This is the one-way path, *bhikkhus*, for the purification of beings, for passing beyond sorrow and lamentation, for the ending of *dukkha* and distress, for attaining the method, for the realization of *nibbāna*, namely, the four *satipaṭṭhānas*.
mn10:24.1: bones disconnected and scattered in all directions: here a hand bone, there a foot bone, there an ankle bone, there a shin bone, there a thigh bone, there a hip bone, there a rib bone, there a back bone, there a shoulder bone, there a neck bone, there a jaw bone, there a tooth, there a skull.
mn10:24.2: He compares this very body to it:
mn10:24.3: ‘This body too has such a nature, will be such, has not gone beyond this.’
mn10:25.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally …
mn10:25.2: in this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:26-28.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* might see a corpse thrown into a charnel ground, bones whitened, the color of shells …
mn10:29.1: bones heaped up, more than a year old …
mn10:3.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body, ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having removed longing and distress for the world;
mn10:3.3: he dwells contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā*, ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having removed longing and distress for the world;
mn10:3.4: he dwells contemplating mind [*citte*] in mind [*citte*], ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having removed longing and distress for the world;
mn10:3.5: he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas*, ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having removed longing and distress for the world.
mn10:30.1: bones rotted and crumbled to powder.
mn10:30.2: He compares this very body to it:
mn10:30.3: ‘This body too has such a nature, will be such, has not gone beyond this.’
mn10:31.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally, or he dwells contemplating body in body externally, or he dwells contemplating body in body both internally and externally;
mn10:31.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of passing away in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and passing away in the body.
mn10:31.3: Or else *sati* is established in him: ‘There is body.’ This is merely for the extent of knowledge and the extent of recollection; and he dwells independent, and he does not take up anything in the world.
mn10:31.4: In this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:32.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā*?
mn10:32.10: When experiencing non-worldly neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, he understands, ‘I experience non-worldly neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, when experiencing pleasant *vedanā*, a *bhikkhu* understands, ‘I experience pleasant *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.3: When experiencing painful *vedanā*, he understands, ‘I experience painful *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.4: When experiencing neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, he understands, ‘I experience neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.5: When experiencing worldly pleasant *vedanā*, he understands, ‘I experience worldly pleasant *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.6: When experiencing non-worldly pleasant *vedanā*, he understands, ‘I experience non-worldly pleasant *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.7: When experiencing worldly painful *vedanā*, he understands, ‘I experience worldly painful *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.8: When experiencing non-worldly painful *vedanā*, he understands, ‘I experience non-worldly painful *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.9: When experiencing worldly neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, he understands, ‘I experience worldly neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*.’
mn10:33.1: Thus he dwells contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā* internally, or he dwells contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā* externally, or he dwells contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā* both internally and externally;
mn10:33.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *vedanā*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of passing away in *vedanā*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and passing away in *vedanā*.
mn10:33.3: Or else *sati* is established in him: ‘There is *vedanā*.’ This is merely for the extent of knowledge and the extent of recollection; and he dwells independent, and he does not take up anything in the world.
mn10:33.4: In this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā*.
mn10:34.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating mind [*citte*] in mind [*citte*]?
mn10:34.10: He understands expanded mind [*mahaggataṁ cittaṁ*] as ‘expanded mind’ [*mahaggataṁ cittaṁ*].
mn10:34.11: He understands unexpanded mind [*amahaggataṁ cittaṁ*] as ‘unexpanded mind’ [*amahaggataṁ cittaṁ*].
mn10:34.12: He understands surpassable mind [*sauttaraṁ cittaṁ*] as ‘surpassable mind’ [*sauttaraṁ cittaṁ*].
mn10:34.13: He understands unsurpassable mind [*anuttaraṁ cittaṁ*] as ‘unsurpassable mind’ [*anuttaraṁ cittaṁ*].
mn10:34.14: He understands mind endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhitaṁ cittaṁ*] as ‘mind endowed with *samādhi*’ [*samāhitaṁ cittaṁ*].
mn10:34.15: He understands mind not endowed with *samādhi* [*asamāhitaṁ cittaṁ*] as ‘mind not endowed with *samādhi*’ [*asamāhitaṁ cittaṁ*].
mn10:34.16: He understands liberated mind [*vimuttaṁ cittaṁ*] as ‘liberated mind’ [*vimuttaṁ cittaṁ*].
mn10:34.17: He understands unliberated mind [*avimuttaṁ cittaṁ*] as ‘unliberated mind’ [*avimuttaṁ cittaṁ*].
mn10:34.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* understands mind with greed [*sarāgaṁ cittaṁ*] as ‘mind with greed’ [*sarāgaṁ cittaṁ*].
mn10:34.3: He understands mind free of greed [*vītarāgaṁ cittaṁ*] as ‘mind free of greed’ [*vītarāgaṁ cittaṁ*].
mn10:34.4: He understands mind with hate [*sadosaṁ cittaṁ*] as ‘mind with hate’ [*sadosaṁ cittaṁ*].
mn10:34.5: He understands mind free of hate [*vītadosaṁ cittaṁ*] as ‘mind free of hate’ [*vītadosaṁ cittaṁ*].
mn10:34.6: He understands mind with delusion [*samohaṁ cittaṁ*] as ‘mind with delusion’ [*samohaṁ cittaṁ*].
mn10:34.7: He understands mind free of delusion [*vītamohaṁ cittaṁ*] as ‘mind free of delusion’ [*vītamohaṁ cittaṁ*].
mn10:34.8: He understands contracted mind [*saṅkhittaṁ cittaṁ*] as ‘contracted mind’ [*saṅkhittaṁ cittaṁ*].
mn10:34.9: He understands distracted mind [*vikkhittaṁ cittaṁ*] as ‘distracted mind’ [*vikkhittaṁ cittaṁ*].
mn10:35.1: Thus he dwells contemplating mind [*citte*] in mind [*citte*] internally, or he dwells contemplating mind [*citte*] in mind [*citte*] externally, or he dwells contemplating mind [*citte*] in mind [*citte*] both internally and externally;
mn10:35.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in mind [*cittasmiṁ*], or he dwells contemplating the nature of passing away in mind [*cittasmiṁ*], or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and passing away in mind [*cittasmiṁ*].
mn10:35.3: Or else *sati* is established in him: ‘There is mind’ [*cittaṁ*]. This is merely for the extent of knowledge and the extent of recollection; and he dwells independent, and he does not take up anything in the world.
mn10:35.4: In this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating mind [*citte*] in mind [*citte*].
mn10:36.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas*?
mn10:36.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the five hindrances.
mn10:36.3: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the five hindrances?
mn10:36.4: Here, *bhikkhus*, when sensual desire is present within, a *bhikkhu* understands, ‘Sensual desire is present within me’; when sensual desire is absent within, he understands, ‘Sensual desire is absent within me’; and he understands how unarisen sensual desire arises, how arisen sensual desire is abandoned, and how abandoned sensual desire does not arise in the future.
mn10:36.5: When ill will is present within, he understands, ‘Ill will is present within me’; when ill will is absent within, he understands, ‘Ill will is absent within me’; and he understands how unarisen ill will arises, how arisen ill will is abandoned, and how abandoned ill will does not arise in the future.
mn10:36.6: When sloth and torpor are present within, he understands, ‘Sloth and torpor are present within me’; when sloth and torpor are absent within, he understands, ‘Sloth and torpor are absent within me’; and he understands how unarisen sloth and torpor arise, how arisen sloth and torpor are abandoned, and how abandoned sloth and torpor do not arise in the future.
mn10:36.7: When restlessness and remorse are present within, he understands, ‘Restlessness and remorse are present within me’; when restlessness and remorse are absent within, he understands, ‘Restlessness and remorse are absent within me’; and he understands how unarisen restlessness and remorse arise, how arisen restlessness and remorse are abandoned, and how abandoned restlessness and remorse do not arise in the future.
mn10:36.8: When doubt is present within, he understands, ‘Doubt is present within me’; when doubt is absent within, he understands, ‘Doubt is absent within me’; and he understands how unarisen doubt arises, how arisen doubt is abandoned, and how abandoned doubt does not arise in the future.
mn10:37.1: Thus he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* internally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* externally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* both internally and externally;
mn10:37.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of passing away in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and passing away in *dhammas*.
mn10:37.3: Or else *sati* is established in him: ‘There are *dhammas*.’ This is merely for the extent of knowledge and the extent of recollection; and he dwells independent, and he does not take up anything in the world.
mn10:37.4: In this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the five hindrances.
mn10:38.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the five aggregates subject to *upādāna*.
mn10:38.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the five aggregates subject to *upādāna*?
mn10:38.4: ‘Such is form [*rūpaṁ*], such is the arising of form [*rūpassa*], such is the ending of form [*rūpassa*];
mn10:38.5: such is *vedanā*, such is the arising of *vedanā*, such is the ending of *vedanā*;
mn10:38.6: such is *saññā*, such is the arising of *saññā*, such is the ending of *saññā*;
mn10:38.7: such are *saṅkhāras*, such is the arising of *saṅkhāras*, such is the ending of *saṅkhāras*;
mn10:38.8: such is *viññāṇa*, such is the arising of *viññāṇa*, such is the ending of *viññāṇa*’;
mn10:39.1: thus he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* internally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* externally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* both internally and externally;
mn10:39.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of passing away in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and passing away in *dhammas*.
mn10:39.3: Or else *sati* is established in him: ‘There are *dhammas*.’ This is merely for the extent of knowledge and the extent of recollection; and he dwells independent, and he does not take up anything in the world.
mn10:39.4: In this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the five aggregates subject to *upādāna*.
mn10:4.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating body in body?
mn10:4.10: he trains thus: ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe in’; he trains thus: ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe out’;
mn10:4.11: he trains thus: ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra*, I will breathe in’; he trains thus: ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra*, I will breathe out.’
mn10:4.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, gone to the wilderness, or gone to the root of a tree, or gone to an empty dwelling, sits down; having folded his legs crosswise, set his body upright, and established *sati* in front of him.
mn10:4.3: With *sati* he breathes in; with *sati* he breathes out.
mn10:4.4: Breathing in long, he understands, ‘I breathe in long’; or breathing out long, he understands, ‘I breathe out long’;
mn10:4.5: breathing in short, he understands, ‘I breathe in short’; or breathing out short, he understands, ‘I breathe out short.’
mn10:4.6: He trains thus: ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe in’; he trains thus: ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe out.’
mn10:4.7: He trains thus: ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra*, I will breathe in’; he trains thus: ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra*, I will breathe out.’
mn10:4.8: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a skilled turner or a turner’s apprentice, when making a long turn, understands, ‘I make a long turn,’ or when making a short turn, understands, ‘I make a short turn’;
mn10:4.9: so too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, breathing in long, understands, ‘I breathe in long’; or breathing out long, understands, ‘I breathe out long’; breathing in short, understands, ‘I breathe in short’; or breathing out short, understands, ‘I breathe out short’;
mn10:40.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the six internal and external dimensions [*āyatanesu*].
mn10:40.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the six internal and external dimensions [*āyatanesu*]?
mn10:40.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* understands the eye, understands forms [*rūpe*], and understands the fetter that arises dependent on both; and he understands how an unarisen fetter arises, how an arisen fetter is abandoned, and how an abandoned fetter does not arise in the future.
mn10:40.4: He understands the ear, understands sounds, and understands the fetter that arises dependent on both; and he understands how an unarisen fetter arises, how an arisen fetter is abandoned, and how an abandoned fetter does not arise in the future.
mn10:40.5: He understands the nose, understands odors, and understands the fetter that arises dependent on both; and he understands how an unarisen fetter arises, how an arisen fetter is abandoned, and how an abandoned fetter does not arise in the future.
mn10:40.6: He understands the tongue, understands tastes, and understands the fetter that arises dependent on both; and he understands how an unarisen fetter arises, how an arisen fetter is abandoned, and how an abandoned fetter does not arise in the future.
mn10:40.7: He understands the body, understands tangibles, and understands the fetter that arises dependent on both; and he understands how an unarisen fetter arises, how an arisen fetter is abandoned, and how an abandoned fetter does not arise in the future.
mn10:40.8: He understands the mind, understands *dhammas*, and understands the fetter that arises dependent on both; and he understands how an unarisen fetter arises, how an arisen fetter is abandoned, and how an abandoned fetter does not arise in the future.
mn10:41.1: Thus he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* internally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* externally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* both internally and externally;
mn10:41.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of passing away in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and passing away in *dhammas*.
mn10:41.3: Or else *sati* is established in him: ‘There are *dhammas*.’ This is merely for the extent of knowledge and the extent of recollection; and he dwells independent, and he does not take up anything in the world.
mn10:41.4: In this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the six internal and external dimensions [*āyatanesu*].
mn10:42.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the seven *bojjhaṅgas*.
mn10:42.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the seven *bojjhaṅgas*?
mn10:42.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, when the *sati* *bojjhaṅga* is present within, a *bhikkhu* understands, ‘The *sati* *bojjhaṅga* is present within me’; when the *sati* *bojjhaṅga* is absent within, he understands, ‘The *sati* *bojjhaṅga* is absent within me’; and he understands how the unarisen *sati* *bojjhaṅga* arises, and how the arisen *sati* *bojjhaṅga* reaches fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.4: When the investigation-of-*dhammas* *bojjhaṅga* is present within, he understands, ‘The investigation-of-*dhammas* *bojjhaṅga* is present within me’; when the investigation-of-*dhammas* *bojjhaṅga* is absent within, he understands, ‘The investigation-of-*dhammas* *bojjhaṅga* is absent within me’; and he understands how the unarisen investigation-of-*dhammas* *bojjhaṅga* arises, and how the arisen investigation-of-*dhammas* *bojjhaṅga* reaches fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.5: When the energy *bojjhaṅga* is present within, he understands, ‘The energy *bojjhaṅga* is present within me’; when the energy *bojjhaṅga* is absent within, he understands, ‘The energy *bojjhaṅga* is absent within me’; and he understands how the unarisen energy *bojjhaṅga* arises, and how the arisen energy *bojjhaṅga* reaches fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.6: When the *pīti* *bojjhaṅga* is present within, he understands, ‘The *pīti* *bojjhaṅga* is present within me’; when the *pīti* *bojjhaṅga* is absent within, he understands, ‘The *pīti* *bojjhaṅga* is absent within me’; and he understands how the unarisen *pīti* *bojjhaṅga* arises, and how the arisen *pīti* *bojjhaṅga* reaches fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.7: When the tranquility *bojjhaṅga* is present within, he understands, ‘The tranquility *bojjhaṅga* is present within me’; when the tranquility *bojjhaṅga* is absent within, he understands, ‘The tranquility *bojjhaṅga* is absent within me’; and he understands how the unarisen tranquility *bojjhaṅga* arises, and how the arisen tranquility *bojjhaṅga* reaches fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.8: When the *samādhi* *bojjhaṅga* is present within, he understands, ‘The *samādhi* *bojjhaṅga* is present within me’; when the *samādhi* *bojjhaṅga* is absent within, he understands, ‘The *samādhi* *bojjhaṅga* is absent within me’; and he understands how the unarisen *samādhi* *bojjhaṅga* arises, and how the arisen *samādhi* *bojjhaṅga* reaches fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.9: When the equanimity [*upekkhā*] *bojjhaṅga* is present within, he understands, ‘The equanimity [*upekkhā*] *bojjhaṅga* is present within me’; when the equanimity [*upekkhā*] *bojjhaṅga* is absent within, he understands, ‘The equanimity [*upekkhā*] *bojjhaṅga* is absent within me’; and he understands how the unarisen equanimity [*upekkhā*] *bojjhaṅga* arises, and how the arisen equanimity [*upekkhā*] *bojjhaṅga* reaches fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:43.1: Thus he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* internally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* externally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* both internally and externally;
mn10:43.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of passing away in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and passing away in *dhammas*.
mn10:43.3: Or else *sati* is established in him: ‘There are *dhammas*.’ This is merely for the extent of knowledge and the extent of recollection; and he dwells independent, and he does not take up anything in the world.
mn10:43.4: In this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the seven *bojjhaṅgas*.
mn10:44.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the four noble truths.
mn10:44.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the four noble truths?
mn10:44.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* understands as it truly is: ‘This is *dukkha*’; he understands as it truly is: ‘This is the arising of *dukkha*’; he understands as it truly is: ‘This is the cessation of *dukkha*’; he understands as it truly is: ‘This is the way of practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*.’
mn10:45.1: Thus he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* internally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* externally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* both internally and externally;
mn10:45.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of passing away in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and passing away in *dhammas*.
mn10:45.3: Or else *sati* is established in him: ‘There are *dhammas*.’ This is merely for the extent of knowledge and the extent of recollection; and he dwells independent, and he does not take up anything in the world.
mn10:45.4: In this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the four noble truths.
mn10:46.1: For whoever, *bhikkhus*, would develop [*bhāveyya*] these four *satipaṭṭhānas* in this way for seven years, one of two fruits may be expected for him:
mn10:46.11: For whoever, *bhikkhus*, would develop [*bhāveyya*] these four *satipaṭṭhānas* in this way for seven months, one of two fruits may be expected for him:
mn10:46.12: final knowledge in this very life; or, if there is a residue of clinging remaining, non-returning.
mn10:46.14: For whoever, *bhikkhus*, would develop [*bhāveyya*] these four *satipaṭṭhānas* in this way for six months …
mn10:46.2: final knowledge in this very life; or, if there is a residue of clinging remaining, non-returning.
mn10:46.22: For whoever, *bhikkhus*, would develop [*bhāveyya*] these four *satipaṭṭhānas* in this way for seven days, one of two fruits may be expected for him:
mn10:46.23: final knowledge in this very life; or, if there is a residue of clinging remaining, non-returning.
mn10:46.4: For whoever, *bhikkhus*, would develop [*bhāveyya*] these four *satipaṭṭhānas* in this way for six years …
mn10:47.1: ‘This is the one-way path, *bhikkhus*, for the purification of beings, for passing beyond sorrow and lamentation, for the ending of *dukkha* and distress, for attaining the method, for the realization of *nibbāna*, namely, the four *satipaṭṭhānas*.’
mn10:47.2: Thus what was said, it was said in reference to this.”
mn10:47.4: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* rejoiced in what the *Bhagavā* had said.
mn10:47.6: The Root Sequence Chapter finished, the first.
mn10:48.2: Fear, Without Blemishes, If He Should Wish, Cloth;
mn10:48.4: the excellent chapter, incomparable, is well completed.
mn10:5.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally, or he dwells contemplating body in body externally, or he dwells contemplating body in body both internally and externally;
mn10:5.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of passing away in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and passing away in the body.
mn10:5.3: Or else *sati* is established in him: ‘There is body.’ This is merely for the extent of knowledge and the extent of recollection; and he dwells independent, and he does not take up anything in the world.
mn10:5.4: In this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:6.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, when walking, a *bhikkhu* understands, ‘I am walking’; when standing, he understands, ‘I am standing’; when sitting, he understands, ‘I am sitting’; when lying down, he understands, ‘I am lying down.’
mn10:6.2: Or however his body is positioned, he understands it accordingly.
mn10:7.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally, or he dwells contemplating body in body externally, or he dwells contemplating body in body both internally and externally;
mn10:7.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of passing away in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and passing away in the body.
mn10:7.3: Or else *sati* is established in him: ‘There is body.’ This is merely for the extent of knowledge and the extent of recollection; and he dwells independent, and he does not take up anything in the world.
mn10:7.4: In this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:8.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when going forward and returning; acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when looking ahead and looking aside; acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when bending and stretching; acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when wearing the outer robe, bowl, and robes; acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when eating, drinking, chewing, and tasting; acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when defecating and urinating; acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when walking, standing, sitting, sleeping, waking, speaking, and keeping silent.
mn10:9.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally …
mn10:9.2: in this way too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
